entries.note: A Kansas girl carried by cyclone to Oz in the 1900 novel.
Dorothy's small dog and steady companion.
People of the eastern country in Oz, freed from the Witch of the East.
The northern witch who greets Dorothy and gives guidance.
The eastern ruler whose silver slippers pass to Dorothy.
Shoes taken from the Witch of the East and worn by Dorothy in the book.
A mark placed by the Good Witch of the North to shield Dorothy from harm.
A straw-filled companion who seeks brains from the Wizard.
A man of tin who rusted in place and needs oil for movement.
Used to free the Tin Woodman's joints after standing in rain.
A great beast who asks for courage despite his fearful heart.
A shaded stretch of road with branches, roots, and narrow clearings.
The city where Dorothy seeks the Wizard's help.
Monstrous beasts of Oz, with bear-like bodies and tiger-like heads, that hunt in packs.
Heavy poppy fragrance that sends Dorothy and the Lion to sleep while Scarecrow and the Tin Woodman remain unaffected.
Leader of the field mice who gathers her people to help rescue Dorothy's company from the poppy field danger.
The official gatekeeper of the Emerald City who admits visitors under city rules.
Eyewear fastened on every visitor before entering the Emerald City and not removed while inside.
Visitors declare their business, submit to spectacle fastening, and then pass the gate.
The route to the Wizard's palace where petitions are recorded before an audience.
A city guard stationed near the Emerald City gate in Baum's account.
A resting place in the city where travelers are directed while waiting for palace instructions.
Ruler of the Emerald City who hears each petition separately.
Formal meetings granted one by one inside the Wizard's palace chambers.
Dorothy asks for home, Scarecrow for brains, Tin Woodman for a heart, and Lion for courage.
The new objective given after the first audience: travel west before rewards are granted.
Western ruler named in the Wizard's condition for granting the party's requests.
A palace record book used to log petitions and sequence of appointments.
The western region of Oz, described as yellow and ruled by the Witch of the West.
The western witch who commands the Winkie country and opposes Dorothy's party.
The quest-card directive to travel west before the Wizard grants any request.
A magical compulsion used by the western witch to hold labor and obedience.
The Witch of the West covets Dorothy's silver slippers, sensing power in them.
Watchers and signal keepers set along western routes to report travelers.
The high western dwelling where the Witch of the West keeps command.
The Witch of the West's far-seeing eye used to watch distant roads.
A cap that can command the Winged Monkeys for a limited number of uses.
A powerful airborne host that must obey a lawful Golden Cap command.
The Golden Cap grants three commands to its holder, tracked and spent one at a time.
Winkies kept to work under the western witch, represented respectfully in brief story beats.
The fortified western dwelling where Dorothy is confined during the rescue arc.
A coordinated plan by Dorothy and companions to break from the western hold.
Steadiness and care maintained while under guard and pressure.
Dorothy's repeated resolve to reach Kansas after surviving western trials.
Dorothy defeats the western witch by throwing water in the decisive encounter.
Freed Winkies thank Dorothy and her companions and offer practical aid.
The western hold and surrounding Winkie country are released from the witch's rule.
The next road objective after the western arc: seek the Wizard again.
Winkies offer guidance and supplies to support the party's eastward travel.
A brief, careful account of what is left when the western witch is undone by water.
Dorothy keeps the silver slippers and records their importance before leaving the west.
A chapter marker that closes the westward mission and turns the road eastward.
The Wizard is revealed as an ordinary man using devices rather than true power.
A planned balloon ascent prepared for Dorothy's return from Oz.
Symbolic gifts offered to companions for brains, heart, and courage.
The Wizard admits he is not what his public image suggested.
Dorothy misses the balloon departure and remains in Oz.
Emerald City entry and audience customs continue on the return visit.
The Wizard gives companions symbolic rewards to honor their requests.
After the balloon mishap, Dorothy and friends seek another route home.
